_provenance: 2026-05-20 hand-crafted seed entries for the Thummim 2026 Restoration Dictionary (proposal: .spec/proposals/thummim-restoration-dictionary.md). Three entries demonstrating the multi-level shape — elementary, 8th grade, college+. Future entries will be generated by a substrate pipeline.
_note: Each entry shows what the Restoration corpus does with the word — not what 1828 secular English means by it. Cross-reference to Webster 1828 is shown below each entry where the comparison is illuminating.

elementary.headline: The you that is always you.
elementary.body: Before you were born, you were already a you. Heavenly Father has known you since before the world was made. The Doctrine and Covenants says that the part of you that is always you is called your *intelligence*. It is not the same as how smart you are. It is who you are, deep down, forever.

eighth_grade.headline: The eternal core of who you are — not what you know, but what you are made of.
eighth_grade.body: In the Doctrine and Covenants 93:29, the Lord says: *"Man was also in the beginning with God. Intelligence, or the light of truth, was not created or made, neither indeed can be."* In the Restoration, *intelligence* doesn't mean how smart someone is. It means the substance of you — the part that has always existed and always will. Abraham 3:22 says the noble and great ones in the premortal world were *intelligences* — eternal beings, not eternal *thoughts*. D&C 130:18-19 says: *"Whatever principle of intelligence we attain unto in this life, it will rise with us in the resurrection."* The intelligence we gain isn't knowledge for tests. It's part of who we become, eternally.

college_plus.headline: The eternal substance of personhood — distinct from spirit and from matter, yet related to both; a primary in the Restoration's matter-spectrum framework.
college_plus.body: *Intelligence* in Restoration scripture is a substance, not a faculty. D&C 93:29 declares that *"intelligence, or the light of truth, was not created or made, neither indeed can be,"* placing intelligence outside the order of created things. Joseph Smith taught in the King Follett Discourse that *"the intelligence of spirits had no beginning, neither will it have an end... that is good logic, that which has a beginning may have an end."* Abraham 3:22-23 identifies *intelligences* (plural, particular beings) as the entities God organized — meaning the term names individuated, eternal persons, not an abstract substance shared in common. The matter-spectrum framework reads D&C 93:33, D&C 131:7-8, and Alma 11:43 as a continuum: intelligence → spirit → element → glorified matter, all forms of *matter* (D&C 131:7), differing in *fineness* and *purity*. Webster 1828's first sense — *"the act or state of knowing; the exercise of the understanding"* — captures cognitive ability. The Restoration redefinition is what Brigham Young called *"the substance from which the elements were brought into being."* The cognitive sense is downstream of the substantive one: an intelligence acts — and that act is intelligence-as-faculty — because intelligence-as-substance is what it is.
conference_refs: B.H. Roberts, The Truth, The Way, The Life (1928)

intelligence.webster_1828_compare: Webster 1828 intelligence = 'the act or state of knowing; the exercise of the understanding.' Restoration intelligence = the eternal substance of personhood. The Restoration sense is upstream of the secular cognitive sense; the two are related but the secular usage misses the substantive meaning entirely.

elementary.headline: To get something by really trying.
elementary.body: When the scriptures say to *obtain* something — like the priesthood or a blessing — it means you really worked for it. You wanted it, you tried hard, and you got it. *Obtaining* is different from just *receiving*. To receive is to take what someone gives you. To obtain is to go after something on purpose.

eighth_grade.headline: To gain through effort and desire — not the same as merely receiving.
eighth_grade.body: D&C 84:33 promises: *"Whoso is faithful unto the obtaining these two priesthoods of which I have spoken, and the magnifying their calling, are sanctified by the Spirit unto the renewing of their bodies."* Notice the word: *obtaining*. Not *receiving*. To *obtain* implies desire, effort, and a movement toward something. The Lord asks the Saints to *obtain* both priesthoods — to actively seek them out, qualify, and grow into them. Webster 1828 defines *obtain* as *"to gain; to acquire; to procure; to get possession of."* The Restoration uses it that way: priesthood, blessings, eternal life — all things *obtained*, not just *received*. The active verb matters.

college_plus.headline: Active acquisition through faithful effort, distinguished from passive reception; a load-bearing verb in the priesthood oath-and-covenant.
college_plus.body: *Obtaining* in D&C 84:33 anchors the priesthood oath-and-covenant on agentic acquisition rather than passive bestowal. The verb in the original Restoration revelations consistently carries the Webster 1828 force — *"to gain; to acquire; to procure; to get possession of"* — implying intention, effort, and direction. The contrast with *receive* is structural: D&C 84:35-37 immediately moves from *obtaining* the priesthoods to *receiving* the Lord's servants, the Lord himself, and the Father — different verbs for different actions in the same passage. Cook (2019, *Great Love for Our Father's Children*) extends the principle to temple covenants: *"Couples obtain and receive priesthood blessings and power."* The pairing is the pattern — *obtain* names the seeking; *receive* names the consenting. Modern English softens *obtain* toward bureaucratic acquisition (*obtain a license*) and loses the inner-quality dimension the Restoration assumes.
conference_refs: Quentin L. Cook, Apr 2019 'Great Love for Our Father's Children'
David A. Bednar, Oct 2014 'The Spirit of Revelation'

obtain.webster_1828_compare: Webster 1828 obtain = 'to gain; to acquire; to procure; to get possession of.' The Restoration uses this active sense directly. Modern English has shifted toward bureaucratic neutrality ('obtain a license, obtain permission') that loses the inner-quality dimension — desire, effort, faithfulness — the Restoration assumes.

elementary.headline: The pure love of Christ — and learning to love that same way.
elementary.body: *Charity* in the scriptures is not money for the poor (though giving to the poor matters). It is the *pure love of Christ.* Moroni 7:47 says so: *"charity is the pure love of Christ."* When you love someone the way Jesus loves them — kindly, patiently, hoping the best — that is charity.

eighth_grade.headline: The pure love of Christ — a gift, not a feeling we can manufacture; the greatest of all.
eighth_grade.body: Moroni 7:47 defines it directly: *"And charity is the pure love of Christ, and it endureth forever; and whoso is found possessed of it at the last day, it shall be well with him."* In Restoration usage, *charity* is not generosity (giving to the poor) — though giving is part of love. *Charity* is the kind of love Jesus has for everyone, all the time. Moroni 7:48 tells us how to get it: *"Pray unto the Father with all the energy of heart, that ye may be filled with this love, which he hath bestowed upon all who are true followers of his Son."* It is *bestowed* — given by God to those who seek it through Christ. We don't generate it on our own; we receive it. 1 Corinthians 13 (KJV) is the classic description: charity *suffereth long, and is kind*, doesn't envy, doesn't get puffed up, *beareth all things, believeth all things, hopeth all things, endureth all things*. Webster 1828's first sense — *"in a general sense, love, benevolence, good will"* — is closer to the Restoration usage than the modern 'donation' meaning. The KJV translators chose *charity* because Greek *agápe* needed a word that meant *love-as-gift*, not romantic love or familial affection.

college_plus.headline: Christ's own love — bestowed as gift, not generated by effort; the eschatological criterion of identity at the last day.
college_plus.body: Moroni 7:47 establishes charity as identification with Christ's own love (*"the pure love of Christ"*), and 7:48 establishes its mode of acquisition as bestowal in response to prayer (*"that ye may be filled with this love, which he hath bestowed upon all who are true followers of his Son"*). The verb matters: *bestowed*, not *cultivated*. Moroni 7:47 ends with the eschatological frame: *"whoso is found possessed of it at the last day, it shall be well with him,"* making charity the criterion of judgment-day identity rather than a moral virtue among others. 1 Corinthians 13 (KJV) provides the operational description, and the chapter culminates in 13:13: *"now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three; but the greatest of these is charity."* Faith and hope are means; charity is the end-state — not because they fail but because charity contains them (13:7: *"beareth all things, believeth all things, hopeth all things"*). Webster 1828's primary sense — *"in a general sense, love, benevolence, good will"* — predates the modern semantic narrowing to 'philanthropic giving.' The KJV translators chose *charity* (from Latin *caritas*) precisely because English *love* could not bear the specific meaning of agape — love-as-gift, love-as-grace-channel — and the Restoration scripture inherits the KJV's lexical choice intact.
conference_refs: Dieter F. Uchtdorf, Apr 2010 'You Matter to Him'
Henry B. Eyring, Oct 2018 'Try, Try, Try'

charity.webster_1828_compare: Webster 1828 charity sense 1 = 'in a general sense, love, benevolence, good will.' Sense 2 = liberality to the poor; sense 3 = lenient judgment. The Restoration uses sense 1 as primary — love-as-gift, identified specifically with Christ's love — making sense 2 (giving to the poor) a downstream expression rather than the meaning itself. Modern English has nearly inverted this: 'charity' now most often means donation, with love-as-meaning rare.
